title: Early-stage local chemistry regulation enabling open-circuit voltage of 847 mV in wide-bandgap Cu2ZnSnS4 solar cells - Nature Energy
text:
Download PDF

AbstractWide-bandgap kesterite Cu2ZnSnS4 (CZTS) is a promising top-cell candidate for tandem photovoltaics, yet its progress is limited by persistent open-circuit voltage (VOC) deficits. Previous reports of wide-bandgap CZTS solar cells have exhibited substantial VOC losses relative to the radiative limit, largely due to a mismatch between the designed global composition and the local chemistry formed during early crystallization, where elemental diffusion induces secondary phases and detrimental defects. Here we demonstrate a defect regulation strategy that stabilizes the local chemical environment and suppresses diffusion-induced secondary phases, therefore maintaining the desired crystallization environment and pathway. This approach promotes benign defects over detrimental ones, thereby reducing non-radiative recombination losses. Consequently, we achieve a certified champion VOC of 847 mV (65.3% of the radiative limit) and an efficiency of 12.4% in Cd-free wide-bandgap CZTS solar cells. This defect regulation strategy provides a generalized insight into the synthesis of multinary compound semiconductors.

MainTandem solar cells based on semiconductors with complementary bandgaps represent one of the most promising routes to reduce costs and boost efficiency in photovoltaics1. Whereas crystalline silicon continues to dominate the global photovoltaic market, the search for suitable wide-bandgap top cells (> 1.55 eV) remains at the forefront of research2. Most high-performance candidates are plagued by issues such as instability, toxicity or reliance on scarce elements, whereas wide-bandgap Cu2ZnSnS4 (CZTS) avoids these intrinsic limitations3,4. However, the record efficiency of CZTS solar cells has plateaued at around 11% for an extended period, highlighting a long-standing performance bottleneck5. It is widely accepted that this stagnation arises from substantial open-circuit voltage (VOC) deficits, as evidenced by most previously certified devices with bandgaps above 1.5 eV achieving VOC below 750 mV (refs. 5,6,7). Unlike short-circuit current density (JSC) and fill factor (FF), which can be improved relatively straightforwardly through optical and carrier transport management, the VOC is fundamentally constrained by recombination losses related to various defects8. This inherent limitation makes VOC the most challenging parameter to improve and ultimately the key factor that determines the upper limit of efficiency in most photovoltaic technologies9,10. Therefore, improving the VOC of CZTS beyond its current level is essential to unlock its full potential.Defect engineering in the absorber layer has long been regarded as one of the most effective strategies to suppress carrier recombination11. As a quaternary compound semiconductor, CZTS tolerates a wide range of intrinsic point defects and defect complexes12. Both experimental observations and theoretical calculations suggest that a Cu-poor and Zn-rich chemical environment is required to facilitate the formation of benign defects while suppressing those harmful defects and clusters13. As a result, extensive efforts during the early development of kesterite materials were focused on tuning the overall composition14,15. Whereas this strategy was effective for kesterite with low baseline efficiencies, it did not drive further breakthroughs on its own. Consequently, composition control has received less emphasis over time. This shift does not necessarily imply a limitation of its underlying theoretical understandings. Rather, it may highlight an important consideration that has been overlooked in practice. The local chemical environment at the time and site of CZTS crystallization is critical in determining the defect states and may differ markedly from the initially designed global composition of the precursor layer.Recently, many groups have already recognized the importance of early-stage local chemical conditions, such as the oxidation states of Sn and phase evolution pathways16,17,18,19. This growing awareness calls for a re-evaluation of long-standing assumptions that the local chemical environment before kesterite formation conforms to the designed composition. Notably, due to the thermodynamically driven reaction between Cu and the chalcogenide atmosphere, Cu out-diffusion during the initial annealing process is commonly observed, regardless of whether secondary phases are formed16,17,20. This issue is exacerbated by the fact that kesterite crystallization typically proceeds from top to bottom, making it difficult to precisely maintain a local Cu-poor and Zn-rich environment at the microscale crystallization sites20,21. Therefore, investigating the actual local chemistry and actively controlling Cu out-diffusion are essential to ensure a favourable growth environment for controlling defects.In this work, by strengthening the Cu–S bonding in the precursor layer, Cu out-diffusion is effectively suppressed, preserving the designed Cu-poor, Zn-rich composition at the initial crystallization stage. An environment close to the intended composition facilitates the formation of shallow acceptor Cu vacancies (VCu) while mitigating deeper Cu on Zn antisite defects (CuZn) and band tail states. As a result, bulk recombination is inhibited and carrier lifetime is greatly enhanced. We consequently achieve a total-area certified efficiency of up to 12.4% with a VOC of 847 mV for Cd-free wide-bandgap CZTS solar cells, marking clear progress towards addressing the long-standing VOC deficit.Incorporating Cu–S bonds to stabilize precursor chemistryTo suppress the thermodynamically favourable reaction between Cu and the S atmosphere during sulfurization, it is essential to incorporate more Cu–S bonds at the precursor stage to stabilize Cu. Accordingly, the metallic Cu target in our baseline process (co-sputtering of Cu, ZnS and SnS25) is replaced with a Cu2S target, ensuring that only metal sulfide sources are employed in the co-sputtering process. Further details are provided in Methods. The corresponding samples are therefore denoted as reference and modified samples, respectively.Both the reference and modified precursors exhibit similar morphology and consist of poorly crystalline CZTS clusters, as detailed in Supplementary Note 1. However, the chemistry of their precursor films diverges noticeably. As shown in the Raman spectra of the reference and modified precursors (Fig. 1a), the main A1 mode of the modified precursor appears at a higher wavenumber than that of the reference sample. Such a blue shift indicates an increase in structural order22,23. Moreover, the reference precursor exhibits pronounced broad tails at around 252 cm−1 and lower wavenumbers, corresponding to the B/E modes that are typically enhanced in disordered samples23. Consistently, the modified precursor shows a stronger A2 mode associated with Cu/Zn vibrations, suggesting improved cation arrangement23. The intensity ratios of the A2 and B/E modes to the main A1 mode and their half width at half maximum values are quantitatively presented in Fig. 1b, confirming the above observations. In the modified precursor, these modes are also sharper, reflecting a chemically more ordered environment. These Raman features indicate that the modified precursor has higher structural uniformity, consistent with enhanced Cu–S bonding that stabilizes Cu and thereby suppresses the formation of Cu–Zn antisites. The reduction of cation antisites in the precursor may contribute to fewer tail states and recombination centres in the final film13.Fig. 1: Precursor layer chemistry.Full size imagea, Raman spectra of the reference and modified precursor films excited with a 532 nm laser, with Lorentzian peak fitting39. The characteristic A1, A2 and B/E modes are labelled. b, Intensity ratios of the A2 and B/E modes relative to the A1 mode and the corresponding half width at half maximum values for the reference and modified precursors. The right y axis is broken between 35 and 60 for clarity. c, High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s, Cu 2p3/2, Zn 2p3/2 and Sn 3d for the reference and modified precursors. Fitted components are labelled after Gaussian–Lorentzian analysis40. Surface contamination was removed by a 60-second Ar ion etch. d, Schematic illustration of precursor chemistry, focusing on Cu as the dominant reactive species. Coloured spheres represent the elemental species, and dashed lines denote bonding interactions, highlighting that enhanced Cu–S bonding in the modified precursor limits Cu reactivity.The precursor chemistry is further examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Fig. 1c. The reference precursor exhibits a substantially stronger O 1s signal compared to the modified film. The oxygen may originate from sample synthesis and handling, while the extract source is not isolated. It is important to note that all samples should be subjected to consistent handling conditions. Deconvolution of the spectra reveals two components corresponding to lattice oxygen and absorbed oxygen, with lattice oxygen contributing to a sharp peak in the reference film24. These results suggest that increased Cu–S bonding in the precursor reduces the Cu oxidation. The XPS spectra of other cations further evidence this interpretation. Theoretically, the use of fully sulfide targets increases the sulfur content in the precursor (Supplementary Fig. 4), thereby enhancing the anionic coordination around the cations and leading to a blue shift of their binding energy, as observed for Zn 2p3/2 and Sn 3d3/2. From this perspective, a similar shift may also be expected for Cu 2p3/2. However, the sample prepared with the metal Cu target instead displays a higher binding energy, which should be understood as a result of more Cu–O bonds and the higher electronegativity of oxygen compared with sulfur25. This observation confirms that more Cu–S bonds in the modified film reduce the reactivity of Cu atoms at the precursor stage. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 1d, insufficient Cu–S bonding in the reference precursor leaves Cu atoms highly reactive, promoting their interactions with anions and inducing elemental disorder. Contrastingly, the Cu atoms in the modified precursor can be stabilized by stronger Cu–S bonding.Structured two-stage Cu incorporationNext, we assess whether the more stable precursor successively suppresses Cu out-diffusion before kesterite crystallization. According to our previous studies, CZTS formation typically initiates at around 350 °C (refs. 20,26). Below this temperature, Cu out-diffusion and CuxSy formation are thermally activated, whereas at higher temperatures, the film transforms into single-phase CZTS, accompanied by continued grain growth. Therefore, probing the elemental distribution at this critical temperature is essential to determine whether Cu out-diffusion remains suppressed until kesterite formation. Time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) is employed to analyse the large-scale elemental distribution at this temperature point (Fig. 2a,b). The reference film exhibits a Zn- and Sn-deficient surface region with enhanced Cu signals, suggesting the expected Cu out-diffusion and the possible formation of CuxSy phases. The Cu-deficient bulk region with compositional gradients also supports Cu out-diffusion behaviour. In contrast, the modified film displays a much more uniform elemental distribution. The difference between the two films is also visually discernible, as shown in Supplementary Fig. 5. A covellite-like colour can be observed in the reference film owing to Cu out-diffusion, especially at the edges. The spatial non-uniformity arises from the high reactivity of Cu, where even subtle temperature variations across the sample can lead to observable differences in diffusion rate. This behaviour is similar to that of stacked metallic precursors, in which mass transfer occurs readily under the chalcogenide atmosphere27. In contrast, the modified film maintains a uniform dark appearance, similar to the as-deposited precursor layer. These results confirm that introducing Cu–S bonds within the precursor layer effectively inhibits Cu out-diffusion before crystallization.Fig. 2: Film composition profiles before crystallization.Full size imagea,b, ToF-SIMS profiles of the reference (a) and modified (b) precursor films reaching the annealing temperature of 350 °C. c,d, TEM images and EDX mapping of the reference (c) and optimized (d) films with the annealing temperature of 350 °C. Scale bars, 200 nm. The red rectangles and arrows indicate the line-scan areas and directions. e,f, EDX line scans of the reference (e) and optimized (f) films annealed at 350 °C. The Mo background signals are attributed to the Mo TEM grid. g, Schematic illustration of film evolution from the precursor stage to the CZTS formation stage. Coloured spheres and open circles represent the elemental species and local deficient regions, respectively. Highlighted spheres indicate diffusing or volatilizing species, and coloured arrows indicate the pathways.Cross-sectional and planar-view scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the reference and modified films annealed at 350 °C are shown in Supplementary Fig. 6. Interestingly, the reference film exhibits large island-like surface phases, whereas the modified film shows only a few flaky-shaped phases. These morphological features are similar to those of the CuxSy and SnxSy species reported in previous studies20,28, which is also supported by the Raman spectra in Supplementary Fig. 7. Surface SnxSy phases in the modified sample suggest Sn out-diffusion, although the amount is minimal at this stage. More Sn diffusion and loss is expected at elevated temperatures, as also evidenced by the compositional analysis of the fully processed absorber (Supplementary Table 1). By contrast, the reference film shows reduced Sn loss, probably because Cu out-diffusion and the formation of CuxSy caps suppress Sn migration. The pronounced Sn loss in the modified sample during the sulfurization could lead to humps beneath the surface and discontinuities in the final absorber (Supplementary Fig. 8). Severe pinholes are even present in the final absorber and are clearly visible without microscopic inspection (Supplementary Fig. 9), making the film unsuitable for device fabrication. Therefore, a further optimized process is needed and a ‘structured two-stage Cu’ strategy is developed. Briefly, metallic Cu is used first and replaced by sulfide Cu2S in the final few minutes during the precursor co-sputtering, aiming to simultaneously reduce Cu out-diffusion and Sn loss (Methods section provides more details). This sample will hereafter be referred to as the optimized sample. The optimized sample is designed to retain bulk properties comparable to the reference sample while maintaining surface characteristics of the modified sample, as detailed in Supplementary Note 2. The phase evolution of the reference and optimized samples is confirmed by Supplementary Fig. 12. Kesterite formation in the reference film involves CuxSy, while it proceeds more directly (without detectable CuxSy) in the optimized sample.The optimized absorber maintains a comparable Sn loss ratio to that of the reference sample, as listed in Supplementary Table 1. Moreover, the optimized sample exhibits a more uniform elemental distribution compared to that of the reference sample. As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and the corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mapping of the reference and optimized films annealed at 350 °C (Fig. 2c,d), large CuxSy phases are formed on the surface of the reference sample. This indicates that the dramatic Cu out-diffusion considerably alters the local synthesis chemistry from the designed composition, particularly along the vertical direction, which also leads to SnxSy segregation at the bottom. Such a local compositional deviation away from the designed kesterite precursor may facilitate the formation of binary sulfides, which promotes clustering and results in the non-uniform film29. In contrast, the optimized film shows a relatively uniform elemental distribution. Although minor CuxSy clustering is present, it remains within the bulk. The corresponding EDX line scans, shown in Fig. 2e,f, support the above discussions. The CuxSy phases observed in the reference sample are known to serve as nucleation centres for CZTS crystallization30,31. A very Cu-rich and Zn-poor local environment near the CuxSy can be distinguished, resulting in unfavourable chemical conditions for crystallization. Such an environment is particularly detrimental at this stage, as once the CZTS crystal structure is formed, engineering point defects become more challenging32. Moreover, surface Cu enrichment drives the composition of the remaining film away from the intended design. Conversely, the optimized film retains a reasonably Cu-poor and Zn-rich surface region. Although complete suppression of Cu out-diffusion is thermodynamically unfeasible, this diffusion observed here is relatively mild and, in fact, mitigates Sn loss. Furthermore, our first-principles calculations support the above discussion by quantifying the defect formation energies in the reference and optimized samples (Supplementary Note 3). In the optimized sample, the reduced formation energy of VCu facilitates this favourable shallow acceptor, whereas the elevated formation energy of CuZn suppresses this detrimental deep defect. Meanwhile, the formation energies of the 2CuZn + SnZn defect cluster also increase, contributing to mitigated carrier trapping and tail states in the final absorber13,33.Figure 2g schematically summarizes the reference, modified and optimized films at the precursor and CZTS formation stages. In the reference precursor, the high mobility of Cu leads to its out-diffusion and the formation of an unfavourable Cu-rich local chemistry. The modified precursor stabilizes the Cu but suffers from severe Sn loss during sulfurization. In contrast, the optimized precursor with a structured two-stage Cu strategy enhances surface Cu–S bonding, which effectively suppresses bulk Cu out-diffusion and mitigates Sn loss, thereby sustaining a local chemistry aligned with the targeted composition design.Defect propertiesThe favourable local chemistry established during the initial phase formation stage is expected to yield more benign defect states in the final absorber, even though the final film morphology, structural features and elemental distribution may appear similar (Supplementary Figs. 15–17). Low-temperature cathodoluminescence (CL) is employed to investigate the defect-induced recombination pathways within the bulk CZTS. At low temperature, CL intensity increases and peak red shifts (Supplementary Fig. 18) owing to the freezing out of non-radiative recombination centres and enhanced carrier localization, enabling analysis under low injection levels34,35. At 85 K, both samples exhibit injection current-dependent CL peak shifts, as shown in Fig. 3a. First, the CL signal of the optimized sample appears at a lower injection current, reflecting reduced non-radiative recombination. In addition, the peak position shifts more rapidly with increasing injection current, indicating fewer localized states that can be filled and thus become saturated more quickly as the carrier generation rate increases. The CL spectra of the reference and optimized absorbers under similar injection currents (Fig. 3b,c) both display two sub-peaks, whereas the optimized device exhibits a more dominant high-energy peak at low temperature, demonstrating a stronger contribution from transitions via shallower defects. When the injection level is increased, the high-energy peak in the optimized absorber responds more promptly (Supplementary Fig. 19), which confirms that the density of deeper defects is reduced, making them more easily filled. The corresponding CL images and spectra are provided in Supplementary Figs. 20 and 21.Fig. 3: Defect states analysis.Full size imagea, Injection-dependent CL peak position measured from the absorber regions of the reference and optimized devices. Linear fits to the data within the measured range are shown, with slopes denoted as k. b,c, CL spectra with Gaussian peak fitting41 of the reference (b) and optimized (c) CZTS absorbers at ~85 K under comparable injection levels. d, Arrhenius plots of the AS results for the reference and optimized samples. Linear fits to separate temperature regimes are shown, colour-coded according to the defect type, and the extracted activation energies are indicated42. e,f, Energy-resolved defect state profiles of the reference (e) and optimized (f) devices analysed by AS results. The indicated area ratio corresponds to the integrated area of the shallow defect relative to the total peak area.Additionally, admittance spectroscopy (AS) indicates two activation energies of 60–70 meV and 160–170 meV (Fig. 3d). According to first-principles calculations, these defect activation energies can be attributed to VCu and CuZn, which serve as sources of p-type doping and are expected to have sufficiently high densities for detection13,36. Moreover, the defect energy distribution profiles for both samples are shown in Fig. 3e,f. Although deeper acceptor CuZn still dominates, the ratio of favourable VCu increased from 11.9% in the reference sample to 14.4% in the optimized sample. The densities of both acceptor states are slightly reduced, implying a lower overall hole concentration due to a decrease in compensated defect states37. These results confirm that regulating the local chemistry before CZTS formation leads to a less defective film with fewer deep defects and more shallow acceptors, consistent with the DFT calculations.Device performance and characterizationMore benign defect states contribute to improving device performance. Figure 4a shows the current density–voltage (J–V) curves and photovoltaic performance of the champion reference and optimized devices. As detrimental defects are suppressed, the optimized device exhibits a remarkable performance enhancement, especially for VOC, achieving a champion total-area efficiency of 12.6% (designed area 0.224 cm2, JSC = 21.0 mA cm−2, VOC = 852.8 mV and FF = 70.1%). A certified efficiency of 12.36% with a VOC of 846.7 mV is demonstrated using a standard rectangular mask with an aperture area of 0.2021 cm2 (Supplementary Fig. 22). The sample prepared using the optimized process maintains stable performance after storage in an N2 desiccator for 231 days, as shown in Supplementary Fig. 23. Further operational reliability testing, such as encapsulated thermal cycling and damp-heat testing, could be conducted in future work to evaluate device stability under practical conditions. The built-in potential of the devices was examined in Supplementary Fig. 24, which indicates a larger maximum achievable VOC in the optimized device, consistent with fewer CuZn defects that can pin the Fermi level.Fig. 4: Device photovoltaic performance and characterization.Full size imagea, J–V curves and tabulated device performance of the reference and optimized devices, based on total device area. b, Statistical photovoltaic performance of the reference and optimized devices, based on measurements from ten solar cells per sample. In each box plot, the centre line marks the median, the open square indicates the mean, the box spans the 25th to 75th percentiles, the whiskers show the spread within 1.5 times the interquartile range and points beyond are plotted as outliers. c, EQE curves of the reference and optimized devices, along with the integrated current density curve for the optimized device. The total integrated current density is 21.6 mA cm−2. d, Fitted Urbach energies43 of the reference and optimized devices from the EQE curves. e, Device VOC and bandgap for certified high-performance kesterite solar cells5,6,7,17,21,44,45. Devices with a bandgap of at least 1.5 eV are classified as wide-bandgap devices. The SQ limit and 60% of the SQ limit are provided for reference46. f, Temperature-dependent VOC measurements with linear fits based on data above 253.15 K, extrapolated to lower temperatures to extract the recombination activation energies7. g,h, TRPL decays and fitted lifetimes of the reference and optimized devices under laser excitation at 510 nm (g) and 638 nm (h), corresponding to carrier lifetimes near the heterojunction and in the deeper bulk, respectively7.Source dataThe statistical photovoltaic performance of the devices is shown in Fig. 4b, exhibiting a similar trend to the champion cells. Additional statistics are provided in Supplementary Fig. 25. The efficiency improvement is primarily attributed to the enhanced VOC, along with an increase in FF. Whereas the JSC remains nearly unchanged, Fig. 4c reveals higher external quantum efficiency (EQE) responses in the optimized device, especially at longer wavelengths. The enhanced carrier collection indicates superior bulk quality. However, an increase in the bandgap is observed in the optimized device (Supplementary Fig. 26), resulting in a narrower absorption edge, which offsets the boost in EQE and leads to a less pronounced increase in JSC. Additionally, the Urbach energy EU can be assessed from the EQE, as shown in Fig. 4d. The optimized solar cell exhibits a reduced EU, indicating a lower density of compensating defect clusters in the bulk material. Figure 4e summarizes confirmed high-efficiency kesterite solar cells, including their VOC and bandgaps, along with the theoretical Shockley–Queisser (SQ) limits. Most wide-bandgap kesterite solar cells exhibit VOC values around 60% of the SQ limit, whereas narrow-bandgap devices typically show smaller photovoltage deficits, which partly explains their current higher efficiencies. Whereas reducing photovoltage loss in wide-bandgap semiconductors remains a major challenge, we demonstrate a VOC corresponding to 65.3% of the SQ limit in this work, marking a substantial advancement and unlocking the potential for future development of kesterite CZTS.Figure 4f shows the temperature-dependent VOC and the corresponding fitted recombination activation energy (EA) of the reference and optimized devices. Although the EA is improved in the optimized device, it remains well below the optical bandgap of 1.57 eV. This improvement is unlikely to be fully explained by enhanced interface quality alone. Band alignment effects are also unlikely to account for the increases in EA and VOC, as the absorber bandgap varies by only 0.03 eV and is not expected to alter the spike-like band alignment (as detailed in our previous work38), indicating that the observed enhancement originates from defect states. In wide-bandgap CZTS, where band-to-band transition is not dominant, the extracted EA can hardly reach the bandgap and is instead limited by the bulk quality. This explains the long-observed bulk CL peak at around 1.4 eV for wide-bandgap CZTS solar cells5,26, as discussed in Supplementary Note 4. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decays (Fig. 4g,h) provide further evidence supporting the above discussion. The optimized device exhibits a slower decay under excitation at both wavelengths, indicating suppressed recombination in both the junction region and the deeper bulk. The effect is more pronounced under the long wavelength, suggesting that the improvement primarily originates from the deeper bulk region. The extracted bulk carrier lifetime has almost doubled, increasing from 2.1 ns in the reference device to 3.6 ns in the optimized device. This substantial improvement is not only consistent with the VOC enhancement but also underscores the critical role of aligning the local chemistry with the intended design as an effective strategy to suppress recombination losses in kesterite CZTS solar cells.ConclusionIn summary, our findings reveal that the crucial local chemistry during kesterite formation can substantially diverge from the intended initial designs, often resulting in unfavourable crystallization environments. By enhancing Cu–S bonding, we effectively stabilize the highly mobile Cu species in the system, thereby limiting its diffusion during thermal processing and suppressing the segregation of other elements. This stabilization aligns the local chemistry with the intended target design, promoting the formation of shallow acceptors while suppressing detrimental defects and defect clusters. The resulting more benign defect landscape reduces recombination losses and enhances effective carrier lifetimes. Consequently, we achieve a certified efficiency of 12.4% with a VOC of 847 mV in CZTS solar cells. Beyond CZTS, the insights gained here are expected to provide a broadly applicable strategy for advancing complex multinary compound semiconductors, particularly Cu-based chalcogenides and other systems where elements exhibit contrasting chemical reactivities.MethodsFabrication of the CZTS absorber layerAn ATC-220 sputtering system from AJA International was employed to deposit CZTS precursor layers on molybdenum-coated soda-lime glasses. Around 600-nm thick precursor layers were deposited by co-sputtering Cu (or Cu2S), ZnS and SnS2 targets. The dedicated sputtering system was maintained at a base pressure of ~1 × 10−7 Torr, and the targets were pre-sputtered for 10 min before deposition to remove any surface impurities or contamination. The sputtering powers of the individual targets were tuned to maintain an overall composition with a Cu/Sn ratio of ~1.5 and a Zn/Sn ratio of ~1.1. In the Cu process (reference) using a metal Cu target, a direct current (DC) power of around 22 watts was applied. As for the Cu2S process (modified), a radio frequency (RF) power of around 130 watts was required for the Cu2S target to maintain a similar composition. In the optimized process, the Cu target was used for the first 35 min, after which it was switched to the Cu2S target for the remaining 5 min of deposition. The layer deposited from the Cu2S target is slightly thicker due to the incorporation of additional sulfur. There was no intentional substrate heating during the co-sputtering process. After the fabrication of the precursor layer, the films were annealed using an Annealsys AS-One 100 rapid thermal processor. The samples were placed on a graphite plate inside a quartz container, with sulfur pellets and SnS powder positioned nearby. The sulfurization process was carried out at 530 °C for 10 min, with a ramping rate of 0.5 °Cs−1. The sulfur atmosphere in the chamber was maintained by periodic removal of sulfur deposits ( ~ 10 runs) followed by a dummy sulfurization run. All samples were handled under identical conditions and stored in a continuously N2-purged desiccator between processing steps.Device fabricationThe device was fabricated following the procedure reported in our previous study5. A 10-nm ZnSnO buffer layer was deposited using a Cambridge Nanotech Fiji G2 ALD system. Subsequently, around 40 nm of ZnO and 220 nm of ITO were deposited by sputtering using the AJA system. Metal contacts were prepared using a KJ Lesker PVD-75 electron-beam evaporation system, where 50 nm Ni, 3 μm Al and 50 nm Ni were deposited. The devices were then isolated by mechanical scribing, resulting in a defined area of 0.224 cm2 per cell. Then the sample underwent hydrogen treatment in forming gas at 210 °C for 5 min, as reported previously5. This treatment has become part of our baseline process. Finally, approximately 90 nm of MgF2 was deposited by thermal evaporation using a Daedong High Technologies Solar-Dual Bridge system. The elemental distributions of the final devices are provided in Supplementary Fig. 27.SEM and TEM measurementsSEM imaging was performed using the FEI Nova Nano 450 instrument with an accelerating voltage of 5 kV and a dwell time of 10 µs. The TEM specimen was prepared by a plasma focused ion beam (FIB) process using a Helios G4 PFIB UXe (Thermo Scientific) system equipped with an EasyLift micromanipulator. The specimen was mounted on a Mo TEM grid. A JEOL JEM-F200 TEM, fitted with a cold field-emission gun (200 kV, energy spread < 0.35 eV) and a windowless silicon drift EDX detector, was employed for TEM and elemental mapping.XRD and Raman measurementsXRD measurements were performed using a Panalytical Empyrean diffractometer equipped with a Cu anode (λ = 1.541874 Å). The X-ray source was operated at 45 kV and 40 mA. The scanning range was set from 20° to 80° with a step size of 0.0334° and a scan speed of 0.106°s−1. Raman spectroscopy was conducted using a Renishaw Qontor Hilmer system with laser excitation wavelengths of 532 nm and 830 nm. A 2,400 lines mm−1 grating was used for the 532 nm laser, and a 1,200 lines mm−1 grating was used for the 830 nm laser. The corresponding laser powers were 1.09 mW and 2.47 mW, respectively. Raman spectra were collected with an exposure time of 1 s and 10 accumulations.XPS and ToF-SIMS measurementsXPS measurements were conducted using the ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). The sample surface was cleaned by Ar for 60 s before the data acquisition. The X-ray source was monochromated Al K alpha (energy 1,486.68 eV), and the power was 160 W. The ToF-SIMS measurements were conducted using a TOF-SIMS5 system from ION-TOF GmbH, Germany. A 300 × 300 µm2 region was sputtered with 1 keV caesium ions, and the analytical measurements were collected from the central 100 × 100 µm2 area.CL and AS measurementsThe CL specimen was prepared using the FIB process, followed by ion polishing at 500 V using a NanoMill 1040 system (Fischione). The sample stage temperature was regulated via liquid nitrogen cooling. CL measurements were performed using a FEI Nova NanoSEM 450 microscope equipped with a SPARC spectral cathodoluminescence system (Delmic). AS spectra were acquired using an impedance analyser (E4990A, Keysight) inside a dark and electromagnetically shielded cryostat. Liquid nitrogen was continuously flowed through the cryostat to maintain the temperature.
J–V and EQE measurementsJ–V characteristics were recorded using a Keithley 2400 source meter under standard AM 1.5 G illumination at one sun intensity, provided by an ABET solar simulator. The light intensity was calibrated with a silicon reference cell (WPVS, Fraunhofer). Measurements were conducted by scanning the voltage from −0.1 to 0.9 V without dwell time. All tests were performed in ambient conditions at around 25 °C, with no control over humidity. A PV Measurements QEX10 system was employed for EQE measurements and calibrated using a silicon photodiode certified by the National Institute of Standards and Technology.Temperature-dependent V
OC and TRPL measurementsTemperature-dependent VOC measurements were performed with the same source meter and solar simulator employed for J–V tests. The sample was mounted in a continuous-flow liquid nitrogen cryostat to ensure stable low-temperature control. TRPL measurements were performed on a Microtime-200 system (PicoQuant). A solid-state pulsed laser with a 40 MHz repetition rate was used as the excitation source. Excitation wavelengths of 510 nm and 638 nm were used, with average laser powers on the order of 10–20 μW and excitation fluences of around 10–30 μJ cm−2.Reporting summaryFurther information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article.

Data availability

The data that support the findings of this study are available within the article and its Supplementary Information. Source data are provided with this paper.
